title: Team inspects glacier crash site
summary: Team inspects glacier crash site By ROBERT KOWALSKI and NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News reporters Website: Coastal Helicopters Website: Alaska State Troopers See related pages: http://www.alaskaone.com/coastal/ http://www.dps.state.ak.us/ast/ JUNEAU - The Coastal Helicopters Inc. aircraft that crashed on the Herbert Glacier, killing six tourists and the pilot Wednesday morning, "just augered in," according to an Alaska State Trooper who reached the site shortly after the crash. "He went in at an angle. He just hit and went in eight to nine feet and rolled over. The tail was folded over," said Trooper Bill Welch. "He hit and skid about seven feet." Welch said the crash site is on a fairly flat and very stable stretch of the glacier about 20 miles north of Juneau. A team of investigators spent T…

title: Server failure stops e-mail
summary: Server failure stops e-mail Angry customers blast GCI By BEN SPIESS Daily News reporter Website: GCI.net A plumber and a plume of smoke have shut down one of Alaska's larger e-mail providers, unplugging about 30,000 Alaskans from wired communications. After a week of spotty e-mail service, General Communication Inc. shut down the companies' mail server for its GCI.net service on Wednesday afternoon. Programmers hoped to have the system repaired by early today, said David Morris, GCI spokesman. Customers of GCI's booming e-mail business lashed out at the company, posting more than 80 messages on a corporate chat board in the past two days, calling the software meltdown "unforgivable" and a "a mail fiasco." The blame, said Anchorage-based GCI, belongs with a plumber working in the Raspberry …

title: Three years after fire, forest is reborn
summary: Three years after fire, forest is reborn By S.J. KOMARNITSKY Daily News Mat-Su Bureau BIG LAKE - Glen Holt remembers inching his green state truck forward on Beaver Lakes Road through smoke so thick he could barely see. A wall of flame crested overhead. He felt his beard start to crinkle from the heat. "I was thinking, 'Whoa, this is bad. This is bad!' " said Holt, a state forestry official who has spent 20 years fighting forest fires across the country. Three years later, the Big Lake fire remains etched in his memory. But Holt doesn't need picture books to recall the details. He only has to glance at the tree canopy along that road where he sneaked through the flames. The torched trees look like black toothpicks, devoid of branches and the leaves that fill the spaces everywhere else in t…

title: Planning, fast action defeat blaze in Homer
summary: Planning, fast action defeat blaze in Homer By TOM KIZZIA Daily News reporter HOMER - State firefighters used picks and hoses Saturday to slap down the remains of a wildfire that raced the previous night through 75 acres of beetle-killed forest on a populated hillside east of Homer. Friday's furious firefight, probably the first of its kind in Alaska's new beetle-kill era, proved a remarkable success. As many as 50 homes were threatened, with several dozen inside the perimeter of the fire. But only one mobile home was lost, and no one was injured. Firefighters attributed much of the success to preparations by homeowners who had cut trees and brush to create "defensible space" around their homes. "Defensible space saved at least 20 homes," said Terry Anderson, the state's incident commander…

title: Firefighters gain ground
summary: Firefighters gain ground Homer evacuates dozens of homes before crews contain blaze By TOM KIZZIA and DON HUNTER Daily News reporters HOMER - High winds carried a small brush fire into beetle-killed timber east of Homer Friday evening, burning an estimated 200 acres of dead forest before firefighters stamped down the blaze at dusk. Some 30 homes were in the perimeter of the fire, but fire officials said only one mobile home appeared to have been destroyed. Firefighters with small mobile water tankers were able to protect the houses thanks to earlier efforts by landowners to clear "defensible space" around their homes, said Gary Thomas, spokesman for the Homer Volunteer Fire Department. While fire department officials in Homer said they estimated the fire had burned at least 200 acres, stat…

title: Salmon stalemate ends
summary: Salmon stalemate ends U.S., Canada set flexible quotas By HAL SPENCER The Associated Press SEATTLE - After years of acrimony, Canada and the United States announced a new agreement Thursday to jointly manage Pacific wild salmon runs with an emphasis on conserving and rebuilding dwindling stocks rather than simply dividing what's left of the runs. In a joint statement, Secretary of State Madeleine Albright and Canadian Foreign Minister Lloyd Axworthy called it "a victory for all those on both sides of the border interested in salmon conservation and the long-term viability of our salmon industries." "We absolutely had to move salmon management between the two countries to a fish-first conservation approach and equitably sharing the catch of fish second," Washington Gov. Gary Locke said. "It…

title: Ban buys time for belugas
summary: Ban buys time for belugas Tribal governments, hunters remain at odds on plan By TOM KIZZIA Daily News reporter A hunting ban pushed through Congress last week by Republican Sen. Ted Stevens should put new pressure on competing Native groups whose rivalry has stymied efforts to curb hunting of the declining population of beluga whales in Cook Inlet. The hunting ban, part of a larger budget bill signed Friday by President Clinton, bans hunting this summer until the Native groups work out a co-management agreement with the National Marine Fisheries Service. So far, federal officials say, a voluntary Native effort to stop Cook Inlet beluga hunting appears to be holding. But efforts to draw up a long-term agreement on beluga hunting have been thwarted by a dispute between Cook Inlet tribal gove…

title: Palmer fire is almost contained
summary: Palmer fire is almost contained Eklutna blaze moving through steep terrain By NATALIE PHILLIPS, S.J. KOMARNITSKY and DON HUNTER Daily News reporters Website: Alaska Fire Service (BLM) Website: National Weather Service Fire Forecasts Website: National Weather Service Fire Forecasts See related pages: http://fire.ak.blm.gov/ http://www.alaska.net/~nwsar/html/firewx/firewx.html http://www.dnr.state.ak.us/forestry/fireinfo.htm Firefighters were on the verge Wednesday of taming a Palmer-area wildfire that temporarily displaced 50 families, but they were having a harder time with a hot blaze burning on steep canyon walls above the Eklutna River. The Palmer fire spread to about 120 acres just off Clark-Wolverine Road east of town. Crews laid more than 4,000 feet of hose, bulldozed 6,000 feet of f…

title: Southcentral primed to burn, officials warn
summary: Southcentral primed to burn, officials warn By KAREN AHO Daily News reporter Fire officials suspended open burning across Southcentral Alaska Tuesday morning and put fire crews on heightened alert as dry, windy conditions turned grass and shrubs into virtual match sticks. "You could probably put a match to it and watch it burn," said Sam Albanese, a fire weather forecaster with the National Weather Service. "And that's in live plants." Tuesday morning, before the first of several fires were reported in the area, the Alaska Division of Forestry began diverting resources to the region, shipping a fire-retardant air tanker from the Lower 48 before schedule, putting Fairbanks-based smoke jumpers on alert, hiring two emergency firefighting crews, and holding back a McGrath-bound helicopter. Off…

title: Teens held in school-site fires
summary: Teens held in school-site fires By PETER PORCO Daily News reporter Two 15-year-old boys were arrested Monday in the vandalizing of heavy trucks - causing up to $100,000 in damage - at the Russian Jack Elementary School construction site over the weekend, authorities said. The teenagers, accused of setting several trucks ablaze, were each charged with arson and burglary and taken to McLaughlin Youth Center, said Anchorage police spokesman Ron McGee. Their names were not released. "I'm going to file a civil lawsuit against them," said Al Crosswhite, general superintendent of K.C. Alaska, the subcontractor that owns the construction equipment. "You know, this (vandalism) has to stop." Crosswhite said one dump truck was burned to its frame, the interior of another was heavily damaged, the seat…

title: Firefighters defeat blaze
summary: Firefighters defeat blaze Dry conditions pose dangers By PETER PORCO Daily News reporter Firefighters doused a three-acre brush blaze Sunday in an Eagle River subdivision where a man tried to burn dead trees and lost control of the fire, officials said Monday. It was the second brush fire in as many days in areas that fire officials designate as critical because of the threat to life or property. Like Saturday's one-acre brush fire on the Hillside, the Eagle River fire occurred in conditions where fires can spread quickly. Because of dry conditions, with flammable dead grass undampened by the season's new growth, the fire hazard is increasing, said John See, a fire management officer for the state Division of Forestry. "It think it's fairly typical for this time of year," he said. Sunday's…

title: Feds plan to auction Mat-Su home seized in marijuana bust
summary: Feds plan to auction Mat-Su home seized in marijuana bust By S.J. KOMARNITSKY Daily News Mat-Su Bureau WASILLA - The only hint the blue-trimmed three-story home on Bartlett Circle is being sold in anything but the usual manner is the sign in the front yard. Instead of sporting the name and colors of a local real estate firm, this one reads "U.S. Department of Treasury." Until a year ago, the house inthis quiet cul-de-sac off the Palmer-Wasilla Highway belonged to Ernest and Judy Chase, a seemingly everyday couple with kids. Then Ernest Chase was arrested and convicted on a federal drug charge for growing marijuana. His house became government property and a potential revenue source for police. Today, officials will auction off the house. The three bedroom, two-bathroom house and the three …

title: Stranded boatman found after 3 days
summary: Stranded boatman found after 3 days By S.J. KOMARNITSKY Daily News Mat-Su Bureau The Susitna Rescue A man trying to reach Deshka Landing on the Susitna River got an unexpected wilderness adventure when an ice floe caught up his boat and swept him miles downstream and into Cook Inlet. When Tom Beltran tried to make his way back upstream, his boat ran out of gas and he spent three days lost in the marshy wilds near Flat Horn Lake. He was in a boggy area about three miles from his boat when Alaska State Troopers found him Tuesday afternoon. Beltran, 42, told rescuers he drank swamp water to survive and fired his rifle so many times to scare away bears that he ran out of ammunition. He was tired, dehydrated and cold. He'd spent two nights chilly sleeping outside without a tent or sleeping bag …

title: Fill-in nurses not up to speed yet
summary: Fill-in nurses not up to speed yet By LISA DEMER Daily News reporter Is patient care being compromised by the nurses' strike? Providence Alaska Medical Center officials say they've seen no sign of that, nor have state hospital inspectors. But a prominent anesthesiologist says that her time in the operating rooms during the first two days of the walkout has convinced her the hospital is not running as smoothly as administrators contend. Dr. Lorraine Kottra has worked 27 years as a Providence anesthesiologist and is a former chief of staff for the Anchorage hospital. In the operating rooms Thursday and Friday, replacement nurses brought in by Providence for the strike didn't know where all the supplies were or how the equipment worked. Yet they would be expected to react quickly if complicat…

title: Checkpoints offer relief to chilled Iditarod teams
summary: Checkpoints offer relief to chilled Iditarod teams By S.J. KOMARNITSKY Daily News reporter See Related File See related pages: http://www.adn.com/pf/mushing http://www.iditarod.com/ http://www.adn.com/iditarod/ NIKOLAI - Jim Lanier never felt the cold seep into his feet as he mushed his team across the desolate Farewell Burn and up the Kuskokwim River into this small village. The minus-40 temperature worked its way in quietly, ebbing the feeling from his toes and turning once-malleable skin into a numb, hard mass. By the time he pulled into the checkpoint here Tuesday evening, his toes were cold, black lumps. "I never felt anything," he said as he sat in the village's community hall eating a cheeseburger and sipping on a juice drink. Dressed in black polypropylene pants and top, he sat on …

title: Recover plan has worked, eagle specialist believes
summary: Recover plan has worked, eagle specialist believes By MATTHEW YI The Associated Press MILLERTON LAKE, Calif. - As the sun slowly rises over the Sierra, a bald eagle perched on top of a tall pine tree eyes its prey far below. In a split second, the eagle stretches his 6-foot wings and swoops down to the edge of the lake. The bird tries to grab a dead fish using his outstretched claws. In a flash, he's caught in a net that pops up out of the sand and over his head. Wildlife biologists jump into their boats and zip toward the majestic bird, quickly placing a leather hood over its head and purple cotton socks over the claws. "Gee, dude, you're a pig," biologist Ron Jackman says, noticing a large undigested lump in the eagle's upper chest. "We think he's the local, territorial male. There's a p…

title: Hilltop ski plan wins city's OK
summary: Hilltop ski plan wins city's OK Nordic trail users oppose change By NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News reporter Proposed expansion of Hilltop Ski Area After more than two hours of public testimony and an hour of discussion, the city Parks and Recreation Commission late Thursday gave final approval to a plan to expand Hilltop Ski Area. But not without conditions. The ski area operators must begin the $5.5 million expansion within five years or the commission's approval will expire. The operators also must get city approval for each phase of the expansion and provide proof they have the financing to pay for it. The two phases are: building a new lodge and parking lot, and cutting down trees for new ski runs and a new chair lift. That phase would involve replacing the Spencer Loop, a popular Nordic …

title: Copyright © 1998 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: Gravy train Army Chinooks fly donated salmon to villages' sled dogs By JIM CLARKE The Associated Press It's a sure bet the sled dogs didn't identify the distinctive whump-whump of the U.S. Army's Chinook helicopters as gravy trains. But that's what they were, flying frozen salmon to villages that saw meager harvests last summer. Communities in Alaska's Interior still use dog sleds for transportation and freight-hauling, and it's the summertime catch of salmon in the Yukon River drainage that powers those dogs. Last summer, however, only about 400,000 chum salmon entered the Yukon from the Bering Sea - less than half an average year's run. So this week the Army's big twin-rotored Chinooks flew out of Fort Wainwright, near Fairbanks, to remote villages with their holds full of frozen salmon.…

title: Night-vision training benefits Bush
summary: Night-vision training benefits Bush By T.C. MITCHELL Daily News reporter FORT WAINWRIGHT - A couple of hundred dogs, maybe more, didn't get the Thanksgiving dinner the Army had hoped to deliver Wednesday morning. The folks around the northern hump of the Yukon River - Venetie, Chalkyitsik and Birch Creek - were waiting, watching the dark sky, but the 22,000 pounds of frozen fish due to drop from the sky never came. This aborted flight was one of many planned to deliver 200,000 pounds of fish to feed working dogs in Interior villages. The effort, which is supposed to resume today, is called Operation Fishex. For the Army, it's a win-win situation. The soldiers get needed night-vision training during the flights, and the military gets to do some humanitarian work at the same time. Part of th…

title: Army airlifts donated fish to villages
summary: Army airlifts donated fish to villages Dogs hungry after bad chum runs By SHEILA TOOMEY Daily News reporter Nearly 200,000 pounds of donated fish earmarked for hungry Interior dogs should be on the way to needy villages next week, thanks to the U.S. Army. "Operation Fish-Ex" will use Chinook and Blackhawk helicopters out of Fort Wainwright to distribute boxes of donated salmon that were born in a Valdez hatchery, flash-frozen by an Anchorage processor, trucked to Fairbanks and stored since September in a fort freezer. Organizers knew the last leg of the volunteer fish lift would be the most challenging - getting the salmon from Fairbanks to the people who need it in more than a dozen isolated communities, most of them off the road system. Private plane delivery was expensive. Mailing meant…

title: Foundation fires worker in porn case
summary: Foundation fires worker in porn case Employee violated policy on company resources use By PETER PORCO Daily News reporter The Southcentral Foundation on Thursday fired an employee charged this week with downloading and distributing child pornography with his work computer. Joseph Charles Darling II was released because he violated a company policy against using foundation resources for other than its business, the organization said. The Southcentral Foundation is the health arm of Cook Inlet Region Inc. Darling worked part time coordinating a program to encourage minority participation in organ transplants. Darling remained in Cook Inlet Pre-Trial Facility Thursday night on $30,000 bail, a booking officer said. The 24-year-old Anchorage man is the first person charged under a new state law…

title: Police snag Anchorage man in Internet child-pornography sting
summary: Police snag Anchorage man in Internet child-pornography sting By PETER PORCO Daily News reporter After an on-line sting, Anchorage police Wednesday arrested a man accused of downloading hundreds of pornographic pictures of children onto his work computer and electronically mailing two of them to an undercover officer. Joseph Charles Darling II of Anchorage was charged with four counts of possession of child pornography and two counts of distributing it, according to charging documents filed in District Court. All of the charges are felonies. Darling, 24, was at Cook Inlet Pre-Trial Facility Wednesday night on $30,000 bail, said assistant district attorney Donald Kitchen. The charging papers tell a tale of electronic sleuthing by police officer Glen Klinkhart. Klinkhart first observed Darli…

title: Aleutian Islands volcano fires to life
summary: Aleutian Islands volcano fires to life Korovin spits plumes into sky, dusts Atka with ash By ELIZABETH MANNING Daily News reporter On the western end of the Aleutian Islands, Korovin Volcano on Tuesday spewed two plumes of ash 16,000 feet above the village of Atka. The village public safety officer reported the blasts to the Alaska Volcano Observatory in Anchorage about 10 a.m. Light winds scattered ash on the village 13 miles south of the mountain. Federal Aviation Administration officials at first were told that the plume had reached 30,000 feet and diverted about a dozen planes en route from the West Coast to Tokyo early Tuesday. No planes flew into the ash cloud, and planes resumed their normal routes within a few hours, said Lee Stratman of the FAA. A local air taxi also suspended ser…
